topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The bouquet sat on my kitchen counter, a vibrant, offensively cheerful splash of color against the otherwise sterile white granite. I stared at it, my jaw tightening so hard I thought my teeth might crack. No note. Not a single scrap of explanation, no delicate script to soften the sheer presumption of it all. I picked up a rose, its velvety petals cool beneath my fingertips, and slowly, deliberately, I dropped it back onto the porcelain dish. "Seriously?" I muttered to the empty room, the sound sharp and brittle. It felt like a deliberate, passive-aggressive gesture, a silent declaration that my existence was somehow incomplete without an accompanying explanation. I couldn't help but trace the edges of the wrapping paper with a rigid finger, a deep, simmering dissatisfaction bubbling just beneath my skin.

[story 2]

When the delivery person knocked, I braced myself for a polite inquiry, perhaps a mumbled apology for the haste. Instead, a cascade of blooms was placed on the entryway table. My breath hitched. I walked over, my posture immediately stiffening. The arrangement was lavish, clearly expensive, and utterly devoid of context. I picked up the arrangement, turning it over in my hands as if it were contaminated. My eyes scanned the wrapping paper, searching for any clue, any indication of the sender's intent. Finding nothing, a hot flush crept up my neck. I set the flowers down with a decisive clatter that echoed in the quiet hallway. "What is this supposed to mean?" I asked, my voice pitched higher than I intended, laced with a distinct edge of bewildered annoyance.

[story 3]

I found the flowers tucked into my briefcase the next morning, the scent overpowering in the stale air of my office. They were an ostentatious display of pink peonies and yellow lilies. I unfolded the paper, my movements jerky and ungraceful. Blank. Completely, infuriatingly blank. I let out a short, sharp exhale that sounded suspiciously like a scoff. I crumpled the paper slightly, the sound of the tearing sound loud in the silence of my cubicle. I smoothed the crumpled paper out again, my gaze sweeping over the pristine arrangement, my lips pressing into a thin, hard line. This was a performance, a baffling display of misplaced sentimentality, and I felt a profound sense of being undermined by the sheer audacity of the unaddressed gesture.
